title: Quiz: Formal Verification of Quantum Programs
description: Test your understanding of formal methods for quantum program verification.

questions.question: In quantum Hoare logic, what does a quantum predicate typically represent?

options.text: A boolean function on classical bits
A projector or positive semidefinite operator

questions.explanation: Quantum predicates are represented as projectors or positive semidefinite operators. A quantum state satisfies a predicate P if P|psi> = |psi> (the state is in the eigenspace with eigenvalue 1).

questions.question: Given a unitary U and postcondition Q, what is the weakest precondition in quantum Hoare logic?
questions.explanation: The weakest precondition for unitary U with postcondition Q is U^dag @ Q @ U. This represents the predicate that must hold before U is applied to guarantee Q holds after.

questions.question: Formal verification can prove the absence of bugs, while testing can only find bugs that exist.
questions.explanation: This is a fundamental distinction: testing samples the behavior space and can only find bugs in tested scenarios, while formal verification mathematically proves properties hold for all possible inputs.

questions.question: What is the purpose of equivalence checking up to global phase in quantum verification?

options.text: To verify that two circuits produce the same physical behavior
To reduce the number of qubits needed

questions.explanation: Global phase (e^{i*phi}) is physically unobservable and does not affect measurement outcomes. Two unitaries differing only by global phase are physically equivalent, so equivalence checking ignores this phase difference.

questions.question: Which of the following are valid verification techniques for quantum programs? (Select all that apply)
questions.explanation: Quantum Hoare logic, model checking, and equivalence checking are all formal verification techniques. Gradient descent is an optimization technique used in variational algorithms, not a verification method.

questions.question: In bounded model checking for quantum systems, what does 'bounded' refer to?

options.text: The number of qubits is limited
The depth of state space exploration is limited
The number of measurements is limited

questions.explanation: Bounded model checking limits the depth of exploration (number of transitions/steps) rather than exploring the entire infinite state space. This makes verification tractable while still catching many bugs.

questions.question: Compositional verification allows verifying a large circuit by combining proofs of its verified sub-components.
questions.explanation: Compositional verification is a powerful technique that breaks down verification into manageable pieces. If components C1 and C2 are individually verified and their interface contracts match, the composition C1;C2 can be verified without re-analyzing the entire circuit.

questions.question: When checking if two quantum states are equivalent up to global phase, what mathematical operation is typically used?

options.text: Adding the states and checking if result is zero
Computing inner product and checking if magnitude is 1

questions.explanation: Two normalized states |psi> and |phi> are equivalent up to global phase if |<psi|phi>| = 1. This means one is a scalar multiple (of magnitude 1) of the other, which represents the global phase difference.
